task: I’m trying to explain to a friend why a part can look fine at first but still cause trouble later in the process, and I want to keep it simple and accurate. Can you help me phrase that in plain English without making it sound too technical?

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a plain-English way to say it:

**“A part can look okay when you first see it, but still cause trouble later if something about it is off enough to matter during use, assembly, testing, or traceability.”**

A few simpler versions, depending on tone:

- **“It may look fine, but if it doesn’t meet the requirement, it can still fail later.”**
- **“Good-looking parts can still be bad parts if the problem only shows up when you try to use them.”**
- **“If a part is slightly wrong in the wrong way, it can pass the eye test and still cause a problem downstream.”**

If you want it even more casual:
- **“Looks fine isn’t the same as is fine.”**

If you want, I can give you 3 versions: one for a coworker, one for a customer, and one for a written note.

user_simulator.text: Yeah, that’s solid. Plain enough, and it keeps the point straight — looks okay ain’t the same as actually being okay. That last one is probably the cleanest.
